item.title: Claude Code 2.1.70: The Most Boring Changelog Is the Most Bullish Signal
item.content:encoded:
Claude Code 2.1.70: The Most Boring Changelog Is the Most Bullish Signal

Claude Code 2.1.70 shipped with zero new headline features. Over 20 bug fixes, a handful of performance improvements, and some VS Code polish. On paper, the least exciting release in months. In practice, maybe the most important one.

What everyone assumed

The AI coding tool space has a pattern. New release drops, Twitter lights up, everyone debates whether the demo is real or cherry-picked. We’ve been trained to expect fireworks from every changelog. A new model, a bigger context window, a feature that “changes everything.”

2.1.70 breaks that pattern.

What the changelog actually says

The changelog reads like a bug tracker, not a press release.

API and proxy compatibility:

  • Fixed API 400 errors when using ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL with third-party gateways — tool search now correctly detects proxy endpoints and disables tool_reference blocks
  • Fixed the effort parameter crashing on custom Bedrock inference profiles or model identifiers that don’t match standard Claude naming patterns
  • Fixed empty model responses right after ToolSearch — the server was rendering tool schemas with system-prompt-style tags at the prompt tail, confusing models into stopping early
  • Fixed prompt-cache bust when an MCP server with instructions connects after the first turn

Cross-platform fixes:

  • Fixed clipboard corrupting non-ASCII text (CJK, emoji) on Windows/WSL by switching to PowerShell’s Set-Clipboard
  • Fixed Enter inserting a newline instead of submitting when typing over a slow SSH connection
  • Fixed extra VS Code windows opening at startup on Windows from the integrated terminal
  • Fixed voice mode failing on Windows native binary with “native audio module could not be loaded”
  • Fixed push-to-talk not activating on session start when voiceEnabled: true was set

UI and developer ergonomics:

  • Fixed markdown links containing #NNN references pointing to the current repository instead of the linked URL
  • Fixed repeated “Model updated to Opus 4.6” notification when a legacy model string was pinned in .claude/settings.json
  • Fixed plugins showing as inaccurately installed in /plugin
  • Fixed plugins showing “not found in marketplace” errors on fresh startup by auto-refreshing after marketplace installation
  • Fixed /security-review failing with unknown option merge-base on older git versions
  • Fixed /color command having no way to reset — /color default, /color gray, /color reset, and /color none now restore defaults
  • Fixed a performance regression in the AskUserQuestion preview dialog that re-ran markdown rendering on every keystroke
  • Fixed feature flags read during early startup never refreshing their disk cache, causing stale values across sessions
  • Fixed permissions defaultMode values other than acceptEdits or plan being applied in Claude Code Remote — they’re now ignored
  • Fixed skill listing re-injected on every --resume (~600 tokens saved per resume)
  • Fixed teleport marker not rendering in VS Code teleported sessions

These are not demo bugs. These are the bugs you find when thousands of developers use the tool daily, on different operating systems, over unreliable connections, plugged into third-party infrastructure.

The performance story

The numbers tell the same story as the bug fixes.

ImprovementImpact
Prompt input re-rendersReduced by ~74%
Startup memory (no custom CA certs)Reduced by ~426KB
Remote Control polling (while connected)From 1-2s to 10min (~300x reduction)
Skill listing on --resume~600 tokens saved per resume
CompactionNow preserves images, enabling prompt cache reuse
Microphone error messagesNow distinguishes silence from “no speech detected”
/renameWorks while Claude is processing instead of being queued

None of this is visible to someone watching a demo. All of it is visible to someone using the tool eight hours a day.

VS Code goes deeper

Three additions that signal where the team sees the primary surface heading.

Session management — A spark icon in the VS Code activity bar lists all Claude Code sessions. Sessions open as full editors, not just terminal panes.

Plan views — Plans get a full markdown document view with comment support. You can add feedback directly on plans instead of switching to the chat panel.

Native MCP management — Use /mcp in the chat panel to enable/disable servers, reconnect, and manage OAuth authentication. No more switching to terminal for MCP configuration.

These aren’t bolt-on features. They’re investments in making Claude Code feel native to VS Code rather than grafted on.

The quiet flex at the end

The last line of the changelog is two words: YOLO mode.

After 30+ fixes for edge cases that most teams would have shipped around for months, it lands differently. Not as a gimmick, but as a team that earned the right to have some fun after grinding through the backlog.

What this means for developers evaluating AI coding tools

Next time you see an AI tool changelog full of bug fixes instead of flashy features, read it more carefully. A polish-heavy release is the strongest vote of confidence a team can make — it means the core works and they’re betting on long-term adoption over short-term hype.

The bugs they fixed tell you more about the tool’s maturity than any feature announcement ever could.

Claude Code 2.1.70 is the kind of release you don’t tweet about. It’s the kind you update to.

Reference links

  • Claude Code changelog
  • Claude Code documentation
  • VS Code extension

item.title: Harness Engineering: Your Job Isn't Writing Code Anymore
item.content:encoded:
Harness Engineering: Your Job Isn’t Writing Code Anymore

Your best engineer spent the morning rewriting an error message. Not fixing a bug. Rewriting a linter output so that an AI agent could understand what went wrong and fix it on its own.

This is what engineering looks like in 2026.

What OpenAI Actually Did

OpenAI’s Harness team built and shipped an internal beta product over five months. Zero lines of manually-written code. Every line was generated by Codex agents. The codebase grew past one million lines. Development time: roughly one-tenth of what a human team would have needed.

They published what they learned. The title: “Harness Engineering: Leveraging Codex in an Agent-First World.” The word choice is deliberate. Not AI engineering. Not prompt engineering. Harness engineering — building the harness that keeps agents productive and on track.

Martin Fowler picked it up and wrote his own analysis. His take: the article buries the lead in its title. “Harness” appears once in the body text, but the concept is the whole point. The tooling, the practices, the constraints that make agent-generated code maintainable at scale.

Eight lessons came out of this work. All of them point in the same direction.

1. Engineers Become Environment Designers

The job shifted. Instead of writing code, engineers design systems, specify intent, and build feedback loops. When an agent produces bad output, the response is never “try harder.” It’s “what capability or context is missing from the agent’s environment?”

Human time and attention became the only scarce resource. Everything else — code production, review capacity, pattern enforcement — can be automated or mechanized.

2. Give Agents a Map, Not an Encyclopedia

The first instinct was to give agents everything. A massive AGENTS.md packed with coding standards, architecture decisions, project context. It backfired. Too much context crowded out the actual task.

The fix: a roughly 100-line AGENTS.md that works as a table of contents, pointing to a structured docs/ directory. Progressive disclosure. The same pattern that makes good UIs work — show what’s needed at the current level, link to depth when it’s relevant.

3. If Agents Can’t See It, It Doesn’t Exist

Slack threads where someone explained the payment flow. A Google Doc with the deployment runbook. Tribal knowledge your senior engineer carries around. None of it exists to an agent.

The team had to encode everything into the repository as versioned, discoverable artifacts. Same practice we’ve recommended for onboarding human engineers for years — write things down, put them where people can find them. Agents forced the issue. A nice-to-have became a hard requirement.

4. Enforce Architecture Mechanically

Custom linters with remediation instructions baked directly into error messages. When an agent violates an architectural boundary, the error message explains what the boundary is, why it exists, and how to fix the violation.

The error message becomes the teaching moment. Enforce boundaries centrally, allow autonomy locally. The agent doesn’t need to understand the entire architecture. It needs clear feedback when it steps out of bounds.

Martin Fowler noted that OpenAI monitored architecture “not only by the LLM-based agents, but also deterministic custom linters and structural tests.” Both kinds of enforcement, working together.

5. Boring Technology Wins

Agents perform better with composable, stable APIs that have strong representation in training data. The latest framework with sparse documentation and novel patterns? Agents struggle with it.

OpenAI’s team sometimes found it cheaper to reimplement a subset of functionality than to fight opaque upstream behavior from a trendy library. Boring technology — the kind with years of Stack Overflow answers and well-trodden paths — gives agents the most reliable foundation.

Fowler suggested this could push organizations toward fewer tech stacks, chosen for “AI-friendliness” rather than developer preference.

6. Entropy Management as Garbage Collection

Agents are pattern replicators. They find an existing pattern in the codebase and reproduce it. Useful when patterns are good. A problem when they aren’t.

The solution: recurring background agents that continuously scan the codebase for deviations from standards and auto-fix. Periodic agents also catch documentation inconsistencies and architectural violations. Code hygiene isn’t a quarterly cleanup — it runs continuously.

7. Throughput Changes Merge Philosophy

When agents produce code fast, traditional merge gates become the bottleneck. OpenAI shifted to minimal blocking merge gates and short-lived PRs. Test flakes get follow-up runs, not blocking.

The philosophy: corrections are cheap, waiting is expensive. Optimize for throughput. Fix problems as they surface. This only works with strong automated guardrails — which circles back to the linter strategy.

8. Agent-to-Agent Review

OpenAI pushed almost all code review to agent-to-agent loops. Codex reviews its own changes, requests additional agent reviews, iterates until the review passes. Humans get escalated only when genuine judgment is required — novel architectural decisions, security-sensitive changes, product direction.

The math: if agents produce ten times the code volume, human reviewers become the bottleneck. Agent-to-agent review handles mechanical checks. Human attention gets reserved for decisions that actually need it.

What’s Missing

Fowler raised an important gap. The OpenAI write-up emphasizes internal code quality and maintainability but says little about verification of functionality and behavior. The harness constrains how code is written and organized. It doesn’t yet validate that the code does what users need.

He also flagged a real-world concern: retrofitting harnesses onto existing, non-standardized codebases may prove economically unfeasible. This could create a divide between pre-AI and post-AI applications in terms of maintenance cost.

What This Means for You

The engineers who thrive in the agent-first era won’t be the fastest coders. They’ll be the best environment designers — the ones who build systems where agents succeed on the first try because every piece of context, every guardrail, every feedback loop is already in place.

Start with your repo:

  • Audit your AGENTS.md. Is it a map or an encyclopedia?
  • Find the tribal knowledge that lives outside your codebase. Move it in.
  • Look at your linter error messages. Could an agent understand them and self-correct?
  • Identify the “exciting” technology in your stack. Is it helping or hurting agent performance?
  • Check if your merge gates are protecting quality or just slowing throughput.

Your agents are only as good as the world you build for them. That error message your engineer rewrote this morning? That’s the new engineering.

References

  • Harness Engineering: Leveraging Codex in an Agent-First World — OpenAI
  • Martin Fowler’s analysis on Harness Engineering
  • Muratcan Koylan’s thread on X

item.title: OpenKoi: Stop Babysitting Your AI — Let the Agent Be Its Own Reviewer
item.content:encoded:
OpenKoi: Stop Babysitting Your AI — Let the Agent Be Its Own Reviewer

You spent 40 minutes yesterday re-prompting your AI coding assistant. You reviewed its output, spotted three bugs, corrected the logic, re-prompted, reviewed again, fixed the naming conventions it forgot from last time, and re-prompted once more. The AI wrote the code. You did everything else.

Somewhere along the way, you became an unpaid QA engineer for a machine. Nobody told you that was the job description.

The contract nobody signed

Every AI coding tool on the market follows the same contract: you describe what you want, the AI generates a first draft, and you take it from there.

“Take it from there” means:

  • Reviewing for correctness
  • Checking for security holes
  • Re-prompting when the output misses the mark
  • Re-teaching conventions it forgot overnight
  • Repeating the cycle 3-5 times per task

This is the workflow that billions of dollars in AI investment has produced: the developer as babysitter.

The missing piece isn’t intelligence

The problem isn’t that the models are stupid. GPT-4, Claude, Gemini — they can all write serviceable code. The problem is structural. These tools lack executive function.

They generate and stop. They don’t plan before acting. They don’t evaluate their own output. They don’t iterate when something falls short. They don’t remember what they learned yesterday.

You fill that gap. Every review, every re-prompt, every “no, that’s not what I meant” — that’s executive function you’re donating for free.

OpenKoi: Executive Function as a Service

OpenKoi is an open-source project that names this problem and builds a solution around it. It calls the concept “Executive Function as a Service” (EFaaS).

The project is a self-iterating AI agent system written in Rust. Ships as a single static binary, zero runtime dependencies, works with any model provider. But the engineering isn’t the interesting part. The cognitive architecture is.

How it works

When you run:

The agent doesn’t just generate code. It runs a full cognitive pipeline:

  1. Sovereign — A directive shapes the approach (“Direct, test-driven, security-conscious”)
  2. Parliament — Five internal agencies deliberate: Guardian (safety), Economist (cost), Empath (user impact), Scholar (correctness), Strategist (long-term)
  3. Execute — Code gets written
  4. Evaluate — The agent scores its own output against rubrics (correctness=9.2, safety=9.5, style=8.8)
  5. Refine — Style below 9.0? Tighten the error types. Iterate again.
  6. Learn — “JWT refresh tokens need constant-time comparison” gets stored for next time

The verb matters. Other tools say “run.” OpenKoi says “think.”

What actually changes

Before OpenKoiWith OpenKoi
agent run "do X" then outputopenkoi think "do X" then deliberation, parliament, output
You see the resultYou see how it decided, not just what it decided
You manually review every outputAgent evaluates its own work against rubrics
You re-prompt 3-5 timesAutomatic iteration, stops when quality threshold is met
Learnings vanish between sessionsPatterns persist locally; skills improve over time
Memory is hiddenWorld model is inspectable: openkoi world, openkoi mind
Locked to one providerSwitch providers with a flag; different models per role
Data on someone else’s cloudEverything stays on your machine

The cognitive CLI

OpenKoi doesn’t just run tasks. It exposes its reasoning. Six cognitive commands let you inspect the agent’s internal state:

openkoi mind dissent is particularly interesting. You can see when the Guardian (safety) agency vetoed something the Strategist wanted to do. That kind of transparency doesn’t exist in current AI tools.

Architecture

The whole thing runs as a single Rust binary. Less than 10ms startup. Around 5MB idle memory. Around 20MB on disk. No Python. No Node. No runtime dependencies.

Provider support

OpenKoi works with whatever you already have:

  • API keys: Anthropic, OpenAI, Google, AWS Bedrock, Groq, DeepSeek, Moonshot/Kimi, Qwen, xAI, OpenRouter, Together
  • OAuth login: GitHub Copilot (openkoi connect copilot), ChatGPT Plus/Pro (openkoi connect chatgpt)
  • Local models: Ollama auto-detected, any OpenAI-compatible endpoint

It auto-discovers credentials from environment variables, existing CLI tools (Claude CLI, Qwen CLI), macOS Keychain, and saved credential files. You can assign different models to different roles — a fast model for the evaluator, a strong model for the executor.

Skills and learning

OpenKoi uses a skill system compatible with the OpenClaw format (.SKILL.md files). Skills ship bundled, live in your project, or get proposed by the pattern miner based on your usage.

The pattern miner watches how you work and proposes automations for recurring workflows. Run openkoi learn to review proposals. Accept the ones that make sense. The agent gets better at your specific job over time.

Learnings persist in SQLite with vector search. What you teach it on Monday stays learned on Friday.

Honest assessment

This is an early-stage project. Single contributor. 65 commits. The architecture is ambitious — Society of Mind, sovereign directives, trust delegation — and it will take real-world usage to know how well these abstractions hold up under pressure.

The self-iterate skill (OpenKoi modifying its own codebase) has hard boundaries: it can improve its skills, providers, and memory system, but cannot disable its own safety circuit breakers or bypass evaluation. That’s a sensible constraint for a system that aims for autonomous iteration.

No Windows support yet. Linux and macOS only.

Getting started

API keys are detected automatically from environment variables and existing tool configurations. No setup wizard required (though openkoi setup exists for diagnostics).

The shift

The question OpenKoi raises is worth considering even if you never install it: how much of your day is spent being the executive function layer for tools that should have their own?

Every re-prompt is a plan the tool didn’t make. Every manual review is an evaluation it skipped. Every “I already told you this” is a learning it lost. The aggregate cost is real — not in dollars, but in the cognitive overhead of babysitting a system that’s smart enough to babysit itself.

OpenKoi’s answer is architectural: give the agent a parliament, a world model, a memory, and quality rubrics, then let it iterate. You review the output that matters. The agent handles the mechanical loops that used to eat your afternoons.

Stop babysitting. Start directing.

Links:

  • GitHub: openkoi-ai/openkoi
  • Website: openkoi.ai
  • License: MIT

item.title: Scrapling: The Web Scraping Framework That Survives Website Redesigns
item.content:encoded:
Scrapling: The Web Scraping Framework That Survives Website Redesigns

You build a scraper. You tune the CSS selectors. Data flows. Two weeks later, the target site tweaks its HTML. Your selectors break. Your pipeline goes silent. You rewrite. Again.

If you’ve scraped the web for any length of time, you know this loop. Scrapling was built to end it.

What makes Scrapling different

Scrapling is a Python web scraping framework by Karim Shoair. It covers the full spectrum — HTTP requests, headless browsers, stealth fetching, spider-based crawling, proxy rotation, a CLI, even an MCP server for AI integration. But the feature that sets it apart is adaptive element tracking.

Here’s the idea: when you scrape a page, Scrapling can store a structural fingerprint of the elements you select. Later, if the website changes its class names, moves elements around, or swaps out its CSS framework entirely, you pass adaptive=True and the parser relocates those elements by matching against the stored fingerprint. Not by guessing. By comparing structural similarity.

This is the shift. Traditional scraping treats website changes as your problem. Scrapling treats them as a solvable engineering challenge.

Installation

Scrapling requires Python 3.10+.

The parser alone:

With fetchers (HTTP + browser automation + stealth):

Everything (fetchers + MCP server + interactive shell):

Docker image with all extras pre-installed:

Fetching: three modes for different situations

Scrapling provides three fetcher classes. Pick the one that fits.

Fetcher — fast HTTP requests, no browser overhead. Can spoof browser TLS fingerprints and use HTTP/3.

StealthyFetcher — headless browser with anti-detection baked in. Bypasses Cloudflare Turnstile and similar systems without extra configuration.

DynamicFetcher — full Playwright-based browser automation for JavaScript-heavy pages.

All three support sessions for cookie persistence across multiple requests:

Spiders: Scrapy-style crawling

For multi-page crawls, Scrapling offers a spider API that will feel familiar to Scrapy users:

Features that matter for production crawls:

  • Pause and resume: Pass a crawldir and press Ctrl+C to stop gracefully. Restart later and it picks up where it left off.
  • Multi-session: Route different requests through different session types (fast HTTP for public pages, stealth browser for protected ones) within the same spider.
  • Streaming: async for item in spider.stream() lets you process items as they arrive instead of waiting for the full crawl to finish.
  • Blocked request detection: Automatic retry with customizable detection logic.
  • Proxy rotation: Built-in ProxyRotator with cyclic or custom strategies.

The CLI: scrape without code

Scrapling includes a command-line interface for quick extraction:

There’s also an interactive shell for prototyping:

MCP server for AI-assisted scraping

Scrapling includes a built-in MCP server that AI tools (Claude, Cursor, etc.) can call to extract content from web pages. The server uses Scrapling’s fetchers to grab targeted content before passing it to the AI, which reduces token usage and speeds up the workflow.

Performance

The benchmarks speak plainly:

LibraryParse time (ms)vs Scrapling
Scrapling2.021.0x
Parsel/Scrapy2.041.01x
Raw lxml2.541.26x
PyQuery24.17~12x
Selectolax82.63~41x
MechanicalSoup1549.71~767x
BS4 with lxml1584.31~784x
BS4 with html5lib3391.91~1679x

Scrapling matches Scrapy’s parser speed while adding adaptive tracking, stealth fetching, and a spider framework on top.

For adaptive element finding specifically:

LibraryTime (ms)vs Scrapling
Scrapling2.391.0x
AutoScraper12.455.2x

Averages of 100+ runs. Source: benchmarks.py.

Comparison with other tools

vs Scrapy

Scrapy is the established standard for large-scale crawling. Scrapling’s spider API is intentionally similar, but adds adaptive element tracking, built-in stealth browsing, and simpler session management. Scrapy has a larger ecosystem of middlewares and extensions. For greenfield projects where anti-bot bypass and adaptive parsing matter, Scrapling is worth considering. For existing Scrapy pipelines, the migration cost may not justify switching.

vs BeautifulSoup

BeautifulSoup is a parser, not a framework. Scrapling replaces it entirely while being 700x faster and adding element tracking, fetching, and browser automation. If you’re currently using requests + BeautifulSoup, Scrapling is a strict upgrade.

vs Playwright / Selenium

Playwright and Selenium are browser automation tools. Scrapling’s DynamicFetcher and StealthyFetcher use Playwright under the hood but add anti-detection, adaptive parsing, and a higher-level API. You get browser automation without writing Playwright boilerplate.

Things to keep in mind

  • Python 3.10+ required. No support for older versions.
  • Browser dependencies are large. The scrapling install step downloads Chromium and related binaries. Plan for ~500MB of disk space.
  • Adaptive tracking needs a first pass. You must scrape once with auto_save=True before adaptive=True works. There’s no magic without a baseline.
  • Stealth is an arms race. StealthyFetcher bypasses Cloudflare Turnstile today, but anti-bot systems evolve. Keep the library updated.
  • Respect robots.txt and rate limits. Scrapling makes scraping powerful. Use that power responsibly.

Reference links

  • GitHub: https://github.com/D4Vinci/Scrapling
  • Documentation: https://scrapling.readthedocs.io
  • PyPI: https://pypi.org/project/scrapling
  • Docker: docker pull pyd4vinci/scrapling

Wrapping up

Most scraping tools treat website changes as your problem. Scrapling treats them as the framework’s problem. The adaptive parser, the built-in stealth, the spider framework, the benchmarks — they all point in the same direction: spend less time maintaining scrapers and more time using the data they produce.

Try the adaptive tracking on a real project. Scrape a page, save the fingerprints, wait for the site to change, then run it again with adaptive=True. That moment when it finds everything despite the redesign is when the tool clicks.

item.title: Your AI Builds Sites That All Look the Same. Impeccable Fixes That.
item.content:encoded:
Your AI Builds Sites That All Look the Same. Impeccable Fixes That.

You have seen this website before. Inter font. Purple gradient. Cards nested inside cards. Gray text on a colored background. It was built by AI, but so was the last one you saw, and the one before that.

Vibe coding in 2026 lets us build anything in minutes. The dirty secret: it all looks the same.

The Sameness Problem

Every major LLM trained on the same web data. The same Bootstrap layouts, the same Tailwind templates, the same UI kit screenshots. Without specific guidance, they converge on the statistical average of “website.”

Ask Claude or GPT to “build me a landing page” and you get:

  • Inter or system font (never anything distinctive)
  • Purple-to-blue gradient hero
  • Cards wrapped in cards wrapped in more cards
  • Gray body text on colored backgrounds (poor contrast)
  • Bounce or elastic animations (looks dated since about 2022)

This is not a bug. It is the most statistically probable output given generic input. The AI is doing exactly what you told it to do. The issue is that you did not tell it enough.

The Vocabulary Gap

Paul Bakaus, the creator of Impeccable, frames the problem precisely: great design prompts require design vocabulary. Most developers do not have it.

You cannot ask for “more vertical rhythm” if you have never used those words. You cannot request “tinted neutrals instead of pure grays” if you do not know that is an option. You cannot steer the AI toward “OKLCH color space” or “modular type scale” if those concepts are not in your vocabulary.

The bottleneck is not AI capability. It is the words between you and the AI.

What Impeccable Does

Impeccable is not a component library or a design system. It is a vocabulary layer. Built as skills and commands for AI harnesses (Cursor, Claude Code, Gemini CLI, Codex CLI, Kiro), it extends Anthropic’s original frontend-design skill with deeper expertise.

The system has three parts:

1. An enhanced skill with 7 domain-specific references

ReferenceWhat it covers
typographyType systems, font pairing, modular scales, OpenType features
color-and-contrastOKLCH, tinted neutrals, dark mode, accessibility compliance
spatial-designSpacing systems, grids, visual hierarchy
motion-designEasing curves, staggering, reduced-motion handling
interaction-designForms, focus states, loading patterns
responsive-designMobile-first, fluid typography, container queries
ux-writingButton labels, error messages, empty states

When you install Impeccable, the AI gains access to these references. It knows what good typography looks like, what proper color contrast requires, how spacing should work. The skill fires automatically when you work on frontend code.

2. 17 slash commands

These are the vocabulary in action. Instead of trying to describe what you want, you use a command:

CommandWhat it does
/auditTechnical quality checks — a11y, performance, responsive
/critiqueUX design review — hierarchy, clarity, emotional resonance
/normalizeAlign with design system standards
/polishFinal pass before shipping
/distillStrip to essence, remove complexity
/clarifyFix unclear UX copy
/optimizePerformance improvements
/hardenError handling, i18n, edge cases
/animateAdd purposeful motion
/colorizeIntroduce strategic color
/bolderAmplify a boring design
/quieterTone down an overly bold design
/delightAdd moments of joy
/extractPull into reusable components
/adaptAdapt for different devices
/onboardDesign onboarding flows
/teach-impeccableOne-time setup to gather your design context

Most commands accept an argument to focus on a specific area: /audit header, /polish checkout-form.

3. Curated anti-patterns

This is the part that surprised me. Telling an AI what NOT to do turns out to be just as important as telling it what to do.

Impeccable explicitly instructs the AI to avoid:

  • Overused fonts (Arial, Inter, system defaults without purpose)
  • Gray text on colored backgrounds
  • Pure black or gray (always tint with a hue)
  • Wrapping everything in cards, or nesting cards inside cards
  • Bounce/elastic easing (reads as dated)

Anti-patterns work because LLMs have strong statistical biases toward these exact patterns. An explicit “do not” breaks the default trajectory.

Installation

One command:

This auto-detects your AI harness and places files in the right location. For Claude Code specifically:

Then open /plugin to install from the Discover tab.

For manual installation, download the universal ZIP from impeccable.style and extract to your project root.

A Practical Workflow

Here is how I actually use Impeccable day to day:

  1. Build the feature with your normal AI workflow
  2. Run /audit to catch technical issues (a11y, responsive, performance)
  3. Run /critique for a design review (hierarchy, clarity, emotion)
  4. Run /normalize to align spacing, colors, and typography with your system
  5. Run /polish as a final pass before shipping

For specific problems:

  • Design feels generic? /bolder
  • Design feels cluttered? /distill
  • Copy is confusing? /clarify
  • Need animation? /animate
  • Shipping dark mode? /colorize

Each command speaks design language to the AI so you do not have to.

How It Compares

Anthropic’s built-in frontend-design skill laid the groundwork. It gives Claude basic awareness of design principles. Impeccable builds on that foundation by:

  • Adding 7 specialized reference files (typography, color, spatial, motion, interaction, responsive, UX writing)
  • Providing 17 actionable commands instead of relying on general awareness
  • Including explicit anti-patterns that break LLM defaults
  • Working across multiple AI harnesses, not just Claude Code

The difference is between knowing design principles exist and having the specific vocabulary to apply them.

Who This Is For

If you are a developer using AI coding assistants and you care about design quality but do not have formal design training, Impeccable bridges the gap. It does not replace a designer. It gives you enough design vocabulary to steer AI output from “generic template” to “intentional design.”

If you have been shipping AI-generated UIs and they all look suspiciously similar — same fonts, same gradients, same card layouts — this is why, and this is the fix.

Links

  • Website: impeccable.style
  • GitHub: pbakaus/impeccable
  • Command cheatsheet: impeccable.style/cheatsheet
  • Case studies (before/after): impeccable.style/#casestudies
  • Creator: Paul Bakaus
  • License: Apache 2.0

item.title: Claude Code 2.1.69: Twenty Small Cuts That Sharpen the Platform
item.content:encoded:
Claude Code 2.1.69: Twenty Small Cuts That Sharpen the Platform

Twenty features. None of them headline-worthy on its own. That’s exactly why this release matters.

Between February 5 and February 28, Claude Code shipped a multi-agent platform — Opus 4.6, agent teams, worktree isolation, remote control, persistent memory, HTTP hooks. Eight releases in fifteen days. Impressive, and rough around the edges.

2.1.69 (March 4) does the harder work: it makes that platform comfortable. Skills know where they live. Hooks know which agent triggered them. Your terminal shows what effort level is active. Plugins reload without restarting. These are ergonomics — and ergonomics decide whether powerful tools get used or shelved.

Skills Ecosystem

Two additions that change how skills work in practice.

/claude-api skill — A built-in skill for building applications with the Claude API and Anthropic SDK. Instead of remembering SDK patterns or searching docs, you get guided assistance for API integration directly inside Claude Code.

${CLAUDE_SKILL_DIR} variable — Skills can now reference their own directory in SKILL.md content. Before this, a skill that needed to load a template or data file from its own directory had to use absolute paths or fragile relative references. Now:

This matters for portable, shareable skills. A skill published as a plugin works the same regardless of where it’s installed.

Plugin Infrastructure

Four changes that make the plugin ecosystem more enterprise-ready.

/reload-plugins — Activate pending plugin changes without restarting Claude Code. During plugin development, this cuts the feedback loop from “restart session, lose context” to “reload, keep working.”

pluginTrustMessage — Managed settings can now append organization-specific context to the plugin trust warning shown before installation. An enterprise admin can add “Only install plugins from our internal marketplace at plugins.corp.internal” directly into the trust dialog.

pathPattern in strictKnownMarketplaces — Regex matching for file and directory marketplace sources, alongside existing hostPattern restrictions. More granular control over where plugins come from.

git-subdir plugin source — Point to a subdirectory within a git repo. A monorepo with five plugins no longer needs five separate repos.

Hooks Evolution

Three features that give hooks better awareness of the platform’s internal state.

InstructionsLoaded hook event — Fires when CLAUDE.md or .claude/rules/*.md files are loaded into context. An enterprise security team can audit which instruction files are active. A development workflow can trigger actions when project rules change.

agent_id and agent_type in hook events — Every hook event now carries the identity of the agent that triggered it. agent_id for subagents, agent_type for both subagents and --agent sessions. A PreToolUse hook can now apply different policies to different agents — let the code-writer use Bash freely, restrict the code-reviewer to Read-only.

worktree field in status line hook commands — When running in a --worktree session, hooks receive name, path, branch, and original repo directory. A CI integration can track which worktree is running what.

UX Polish

Small changes to the daily interaction surface.

Ctrl+U on empty bash prompt exits bash mode — Matches the existing Escape and Backspace behavior. One more muscle memory path that works as expected.

Numeric keypad support — Interview questions (those numbered option lists Claude presents) now accept input from the numeric keypad, not just the number row above QWERTY. If you’ve been jabbing the numpad and wondering why nothing happened, that’s fixed.

Effort level in spinner — The logo and spinner now display the active effort setting (e.g., “with low effort”). Before, you had to check /config to remember what effort level was active. Now it’s visible whenever Claude is thinking.

Agent name in terminal title — When using claude --agent, the terminal title shows the agent name. With multiple terminal tabs running different agents, you can tell them apart at a glance.

Claude Code Desktop prompt — A one-time startup suggestion for Claude Code Desktop on macOS and Windows. Shown up to 3 times, then dismissed permanently. Low friction, easy to ignore.

Configuration

Two settings for specific environments.

sandbox.enableWeakerNetworkIsolation (macOS only) — Allows Go programs like gh, gcloud, and terraform to verify TLS certificates when using a custom MITM proxy with httpProxyPort. Corporate networks with TLS inspection proxies need this.

includeGitInstructions — Removes built-in commit and PR workflow instructions from Claude’s system prompt. Also available as CLAUDE_CODE_DISABLE_GIT_INSTRUCTIONS env var. Useful if you have custom commit workflows in your CLAUDE.md and don’t want Claude’s defaults conflicting.

Remote Control Enhancement

Optional name argument — /remote-control My Project or --name "My Project" sets a custom session title visible in claude.ai/code. When you’re monitoring multiple remote sessions from your phone, names beat auto-generated IDs.

The Pattern

Read the features individually and you see a maintenance release. Read them together and you see something deliberate: every change targets the interaction surface between you and the platform.

LayerWhat shippedWhy it matters
Skills/claude-api, ${CLAUDE_SKILL_DIR}Skills become portable and self-aware
Plugins/reload-plugins, trust customization, git-subdirPlugin development and enterprise deployment get practical
HooksInstructionsLoaded, agent identity, worktree contextHooks can make agent-aware, context-aware decisions
UXCtrl+U, numpad, effort display, agent name in titleDaily friction removed from the interaction surface
ConfigTLS proxy support, git instruction toggleEdge-case environments work without workarounds

The multi-agent platform from 2.1.32–2.1.63 was powerful. 2.1.69 makes it fit your hands.

What to Try

  1. /reload-plugins — If you develop or test plugins, this saves you restarts
  2. Effort level display — Adjust effort with /effort and watch the spinner reflect it
  3. ${CLAUDE_SKILL_DIR} — Refactor a skill to reference its own directory for templates or data files
  4. InstructionsLoaded hook — Build a hook that logs when project instructions change
  5. Remote control naming — Name your next remote session: /remote-control "Feature Auth"

Reference

  • Claude Code Changelog
  • Skills documentation
  • Hooks documentation
  • Plugins documentation
  • Remote Control

item.title: Harness Engineering: OpenAI's 8 Rules for an Agent-First World
item.content:encoded:
Harness Engineering: OpenAI’s 8 Rules for an Agent-First World

The 800-Line File That Had to Die

A team at OpenAI killed their 800-line AGENTS.md file. Not because it was wrong — because it was too right. The agents couldn’t find what they needed in a wall of context.

They replaced it with a 100-line table of contents pointing to structured files in a docs/ directory. That single decision captures what’s happening to software engineering right now.

The engineers getting the most out of AI agents aren’t writing better prompts or picking smarter models. They’re redesigning the engineering environment itself — repo structure, linter rules, merge gates, documentation layout — so agents can operate independently.

OpenAI calls this “harness engineering.” Muratcan Koylan distilled the key patterns from OpenAI’s published practices, and what emerges looks nothing like how most teams work today.

The 8 Principles

1. Engineers Become Environment Designers

The job shifted. It’s no longer about writing code — it’s about designing systems, specifying intent, and building feedback loops.

When something fails, the question isn’t “try harder.” It’s “what capability is missing?”

Human time and attention are the only scarce resources left. Everything else can be delegated — if the environment is right.

2. Give Agents a Map, Not an Encyclopedia

Progressive disclosure beats comprehensive documentation. Every time.

A ~100-line AGENTS.md as a table of contents, pointing to a structured docs/ directory, outperforms a massive all-in-one reference. Too much context crowds out the actual task.

The same pattern that works for onboarding human engineers works for agents. Maybe even better, because agents follow what they can see and ignore what they can’t.

3. If Agents Can’t See It, It Doesn’t Exist

Slack threads. Google Docs. Hallway conversations. Tribal knowledge passed down through pair programming sessions.

All invisible to agents.

If knowledge isn’t encoded as versioned, discoverable artifacts in the repo, it effectively doesn’t exist for your fastest workers. Think of it as onboarding practices — but for non-human engineers who will never ask a clarifying question over coffee.

4. Enforce Architecture Mechanically, Not Through Instructions

This one is sharp.

Custom linters with remediation instructions baked into error messages. The error output itself becomes agent context. An agent hits a boundary violation, reads the error message, and knows exactly how to fix it — without consulting any documentation.

Strict layered architecture with validated dependency directions. The principle: enforce boundaries centrally, allow autonomy locally.

Telling agents “don’t do X” in a markdown file is a suggestion. Making X trigger a build failure is a rule.

5. Boring Technology Wins

Composable, stable APIs with strong training-set representation outperform cutting-edge libraries that agents struggle with.

Agents have been trained on mountains of React, Express, and PostgreSQL code. They’ve seen far less of your team’s custom framework or that niche library with 200 GitHub stars.

Sometimes it’s cheaper to reimplement a subset of functionality than fight opaque upstream behavior. Pick what agents already know.

6. Entropy Management Is Garbage Collection

Agents replicate existing patterns. All of them. Including the bad ones.

That one hacky workaround from six months ago? An agent will find it, treat it as the canonical pattern, and reproduce it across 40 files before anyone notices.

Without active maintenance, code quality degrades fast. The solution: recurring background agents that scan for deviations from established patterns and auto-fix. Continuous garbage collection for your codebase.

7. Throughput Changes Merge Philosophy

Minimal blocking merge gates. Short-lived PRs. Test flakes addressed with follow-up runs, not by blocking merges.

The principle: corrections are cheap, waiting is expensive.

When agents can generate and test changes faster than humans can review them, the bottleneck moves from code production to code approval. Redesign the pipeline for throughput, not for gatekeeping.

8. Agent-to-Agent Code Review

This is where it gets interesting.

OpenAI pushed almost all code review to agent-to-agent loops. Codex reviews its own changes, requests additional agent reviews, iterates until satisfied. Humans are escalated to only when judgment is required.

Read that again. The code review loop — something most teams consider a core human activity — has been restructured. Not eliminated. The human role shifted from reviewing every change to designing review criteria and handling edge cases.

The Pattern Underneath

Across all eight principles, the same thing happened. Nothing disappeared. Everything shifted.

The engineer didn’t become irrelevant. The engineer’s job description changed. From code author to environment designer. From reviewer to review-system designer. From architect to architecture-enforcement designer.

The code still gets written. The architecture still gets designed. The reviews still happen. But the human’s primary output shifted from code artifacts to environment artifacts — documentation structure, linter rules, merge policies, feedback loops.

Where to Start

The question isn’t whether this shift will reach your team. It’s whether you’ll design for it or react to it.

Start with one audit: can a new agent, with zero context beyond what’s in the repo, find what it needs, follow the architectural rules, and ship working code?

If not, you have your backlog.

A few concrete first moves:

  • Audit your AGENTS.md (or equivalent). Is it a wall of text or a navigable map? Trim it to a table of contents pointing to structured docs.
  • Check for invisible knowledge. What do new hires learn in their first week that isn’t written down? That’s your agent knowledge gap too.
  • Add remediation hints to your linter rules. Every error message is a teaching opportunity for agents.
  • Pick one pattern violation that keeps recurring and write a background cleanup script. Automate the janitor.
  • Try agent-to-agent review on a low-risk PR. Set up a second agent session to review the first agent’s output before a human looks at it.

References

  • OpenAI: Harness Engineering — the original post on leveraging Codex in an agent-first world
  • Muratcan Koylan’s thread — the distilled 8-point summary that inspired this analysis

item.title: Paperclip: Your AI Agents Need a Company, Not Better Prompts
item.content:encoded:
Paperclip: Your AI Agents Need a Company, Not Better Prompts

The Missing Layer

You can download a company now. Not a template. Not a tutorial. A functioning business with a CEO, a CTO, engineers, and a marketing team — all AI agents, organized under an org chart, governed by budgets, pointed at a single mission.

One command. That’s it. Paperclip is MIT-licensed, self-hosted, and needs no account. The interesting question isn’t whether AI can do the work — it’s who manages the office.

The Real Problem Nobody Talks About

Everyone focused on building smarter agents. Longer context windows. Better tool use. More autonomous sessions. And the agents did get better. Claude writes production code. Codex ships features. OpenClaw runs for hours without supervision.

But here’s what actually happens when you scale up:

  • You have 20 Claude Code tabs open. You can’t remember which one is doing what.
  • You reboot your machine. Everything vanishes.
  • A runaway loop burns through $200 in tokens before you notice.
  • You spend 15 minutes gathering context just to remind an agent what the project is about.
  • You’re basically reinventing project management with sticky notes and folder names.

The agents aren’t the bottleneck. The lack of organizational structure is.

Think About How Human Companies Work

When you hire 20 people, you don’t throw them in a room and say “build something.” You give them:

  • An org chart with reporting lines
  • Job descriptions with clear responsibilities
  • Budgets with spending limits
  • Goals that cascade from a company mission down to individual tasks
  • A system for accountability and oversight

AI agents need the same things. Paperclip provides them.

What Paperclip Actually Does

Paperclip is a Node.js server with a React dashboard. It orchestrates AI agents into a functioning organization. The setup follows three steps:

StepWhat You DoExample
01Define the goal”Build the #1 AI note-taking app to $1M MRR”
02Hire the teamCEO (Claude), CTO (Cursor), Engineers (Codex), Marketing (OpenClaw)
03Approve and runReview strategy, set budgets, hit go

From there, agents operate on their own. They wake on scheduled heartbeats, check their task queue, delegate up and down the org chart, and work. Every decision gets logged. Every tool call gets traced. Sessions persist across reboots.

Key Features

Bring Your Own Agent

Paperclip is unopinionated about agent runtimes. If it can receive a heartbeat signal, it’s hired. Claude Code sessions, OpenClaw bots, Python scripts, shell commands, HTTP webhooks — anything works.

Agent TypeWorks?
OpenClawYes
Claude CodeYes
CodexYes
CursorYes
Bash scriptsYes
HTTP webhooksYes

Cost Control That Actually Works

Every agent gets a monthly budget. At 80% utilization, you get a warning. At 100%, the agent auto-pauses and new tasks are blocked. You can override the limit at any time.

Track costs per agent, per task, per project, per goal. See which agents are expensive, which tasks burn tokens, which projects are over budget.

Multi-Company Support

One Paperclip deployment can run dozens of isolated businesses. A crypto trading desk with 12 agents. A TikTok content factory with 5. An e-commerce operation with 10. Complete data isolation between them. One control plane for everything.

Governance

You operate as the board of directors. Agents can’t hire new agents without your approval. The CEO can’t execute a strategy you haven’t reviewed. You can pause, override, reassign, or terminate any agent at any time.

Autonomy is a privilege you grant, not a default.

Ticket System with Full Tracing

Every instruction, every response, every tool call and decision is recorded. Append-only audit log — no edits, no deletions.

Goal Alignment

Every task traces back to the company mission. Agents don’t just know what to do — they know why. Context flows from the mission through projects down to individual tasks automatically via Paperclip’s SKILLS.md.

What Paperclip Is Not

This is worth being clear about:

  • Not a chatbot. Agents have jobs, not chat windows.
  • Not an agent framework. It doesn’t tell you how to build agents. It tells you how to run a company made of them.
  • Not a workflow builder. No drag-and-drop pipelines. It models companies.
  • Not a prompt manager. Agents bring their own prompts, models, and runtimes.
  • Not a single-agent tool. If you have one agent, you probably don’t need this. If you have twenty, you do.

Technical Details

Paperclip runs as a single Node.js process locally, with an embedded PostgreSQL database created automatically. No external dependencies for getting started.

The API server runs at http://localhost:3100. Requirements: Node.js 20+, pnpm 9.15+.

For production, point it at your own Postgres and deploy however you like. Solo entrepreneurs can use Tailscale for remote access, then move to Vercel or similar when needed.

Under the Hood

  • Atomic execution — Task checkout and budget enforcement are atomic. No double-work, no runaway spend.
  • Persistent agent state — Agents resume the same task context across heartbeats instead of restarting from scratch.
  • Runtime skill injection — Agents learn Paperclip workflows at runtime without retraining.
  • Governance with rollback — Config changes are versioned. Bad changes can be rolled back.
  • Portable company templates — Export/import orgs, agents, and skills with secret scrubbing.

Coming Soon: Clipmart

The team is building a marketplace for company templates. Browse pre-built org structures — a crypto trading desk, a YouTube content factory, a dev agency — and import them into your Paperclip instance in seconds.

Download a business. Import. Run.

Who Should Use This

  • You coordinate many different agents (OpenClaw, Codex, Claude, Cursor) toward a common goal
  • You have 20 simultaneous Claude Code terminals and lose track of what everyone is doing
  • You want agents running autonomously 24/7 but still want to audit work when needed
  • You want to monitor costs and enforce budgets
  • You want to manage autonomous businesses from your phone

Getting Started

The interactive setup walks you through database configuration and creating your first company. From there, define your goal, hire your agents, and let Paperclip handle the coordination.

  • GitHub: paperclipai/paperclip
  • Docs: paperclip.ing/docs
  • Discord: discord.gg/m4HZY7xNG3
  • License: MIT

The agents are ready. Give them a company to work in.

item.title: PM Skills Marketplace: 65 Agentic Skills That Turn Claude Code Into a Product Management Operating System
item.content:encoded:
PM Skills Marketplace: 65 Agentic Skills That Turn Claude Code Into a Product Management Operating System

You ask Claude to write a PRD. You get two pages of professional-sounding text that would embarrass anyone who’s actually read Inspired. The structure looks right, the words are in the right order, but there’s no Opportunity Solution Tree behind the feature list, no assumption mapping behind the priorities, no JTBD framing behind the user stories.

You know the frameworks. You’ve read Teresa Torres on continuous discovery, Marty Cagan on empowered product teams, Alberto Savoia on pretotyping, Dan Olsen on lean product. The problem isn’t knowledge. It’s that these frameworks sit on your bookshelf while Claude generates flat text at the speed of light.

Paweł Huryn — author of The Product Compass newsletter — built PM Skills Marketplace to close that gap.

What Is PM Skills Marketplace?

65 skills. 36 chained workflows. 8 plugins. All open source, MIT licensed.

Each skill encodes a proven PM framework — not as a prompt template, but as domain knowledge that Claude draws on during conversation. When you discuss product discovery, Claude automatically loads Teresa Torres’s Opportunity Solution Tree methodology. When you prioritize features, it applies Impact × Risk matrices with experiment suggestions. When you test assumptions, it uses Alberto Savoia’s pretotyping approach from The Right It.

GitHub: phuryn/pm-skills Author: Paweł Huryn (The Product Compass) License: MIT Compatibility: Claude Code, Cowork, Gemini CLI, Cursor, Codex CLI, Kiro

Three-Layer Architecture

The design is clean. Skills are building blocks. Commands chain skills into workflows. Plugins group everything by PM domain.

Skills

Each skill gives Claude domain knowledge for a specific PM task. Some are standalone references — prioritization-frameworks covers 9 frameworks (Opportunity Score, ICE, RICE, MoSCoW, Kano, etc.) that Claude draws on whenever you discuss prioritization. Others are workflow components that multiple commands share.

Skills load automatically when relevant to the conversation. No explicit invocation needed. If you want to force a specific one, use /plugin-name:skill-name.

Commands

Commands are where it gets interesting. /discover chains four skills together:

  1. brainstorm-ideas — multi-perspective ideation (PM, Designer, Engineer)
  2. identify-assumptions — risky assumptions across Value, Usability, Viability, Feasibility
  3. prioritize-assumptions — Impact × Risk matrix with experiment suggestions
  4. brainstorm-experiments — design lean experiments to test top assumptions

One command, full discovery cycle. And when it finishes, it suggests the next relevant command — just follow the prompts.

Plugins

Eight plugins cover the PM lifecycle end to end:

PluginSkillsCommandsWhat It Covers
pm-product-discovery135Ideation, experiments, assumption testing, OST, interviews
pm-product-strategy125Strategy canvas, vision, business models, pricing, SWOT
pm-execution1510PRDs, OKRs, roadmaps, sprints, retros, user stories
pm-market-research73Personas, journey maps, market sizing, competitor analysis
pm-data-analytics33SQL generation, cohort analysis, A/B test analysis
pm-go-to-market63GTM strategy, beachhead segments, ICP, growth loops
pm-marketing-growth52Positioning, value props, product naming, North Star
pm-toolkit45Resume review, NDAs, privacy policies, proofreading

Installation

For Claude Code (Cowork):

  1. Open Customize (bottom-left)
  2. Go to Browse plugins → Personal → +
  3. Select Add marketplace from GitHub
  4. Enter: phuryn/pm-skills

All 8 plugins install automatically.

For Claude Code CLI:

For other tools (Gemini CLI, Cursor, Codex CLI, Kiro):

Skills work in any tool that reads SKILL.md files. Commands are Claude-specific.

Practical Workflows

Here’s where PM Skills differs from prompting. Instead of crafting elaborate prompts for each PM task, you get structured workflows that chain together.

New Product Idea? Start with /discover

This walks you through a complete discovery cycle. Ideation across three perspectives. Assumption identification across four risk categories. Prioritization using Impact × Risk. Experiment design using lean startup methods.

Need Strategic Clarity? Use /strategy

Builds a comprehensive 9-section Product Strategy Canvas: vision, target customer, value proposition, competitive advantage, growth strategy, revenue model, metrics, risks, and strategic bets.

Writing a PRD? Try /write-prd

Creates a comprehensive 8-section PRD — not generic boilerplate, but a structured document with the rigor of frameworks from Dan Olsen and Marty Cagan.

Preparing for Launch? Run /plan-launch

Full GTM strategy from beachhead segment identification through ICP definition, messaging, channels, and launch plan.

Defining Success? Set /north-star

Defines your North Star Metric and supporting input metrics with business game classification.

The Intellectual Heritage

What makes PM Skills valuable isn’t the tooling — it’s what’s encoded inside. The skills are built on work from:

  • Teresa Torres — Continuous Discovery Habits (Opportunity Solution Trees, assumption mapping)
  • Marty Cagan — INSPIRED and TRANSFORMED (empowered product teams, product strategy)
  • Alberto Savoia — The Right It (pretotyping, lean experiments)
  • Dan Olsen — The Lean Product Playbook (product-market fit methodology)
  • Roger L. Martin — Playing to Win (strategy choices)
  • Ash Maurya — Running Lean (Lean Canvas)
  • Strategyzer — Business Model Generation and Value Proposition Design
  • Christina Wodtke — Radical Focus (OKRs)
  • Anthony W. Ulwick — Jobs to Be Done
  • Alistair Croll & Benjamin Yoskovitz — Lean Analytics
  • Sean Ellis — Hacking Growth
  • Maja Voje — Go-To-Market Strategist

These aren’t name-drops. Each framework is embedded in the skill’s domain knowledge and guides Claude’s output structure.

What Makes This Different from Prompting?

Three things.

Structure, not templates. Skills give Claude domain knowledge, not fill-in-the-blank templates. The output adapts to your specific context while maintaining framework rigor.

Chained workflows. Commands link skills into sequences that mirror how PM work actually flows. Discovery feeds into strategy feeds into execution. You don’t manually orchestrate the pipeline.

Automatic loading. Skills activate when relevant to the conversation. Discuss pricing and Claude draws on pricing strategy, willingness-to-pay analysis, and competitive pricing models without you asking.

Who Is This For?

PMs who already know the frameworks and want them applied consistently. Founders doing PM work alongside engineering. Tech leads making product decisions without a dedicated PM. Anyone who’s read the books and wants the rigor without the overhead.

If you’ve never read Torres or Cagan, start with the books. PM Skills encodes expertise — it doesn’t replace understanding.

Rough Edges

The commands (slash-command workflows) only work in Claude Code and Cowork. Other tools get the skills but not the chaining. The CSV-based story framework format is functional but not elegant. And like any AI tool, the output quality depends on the quality of your input — garbage context in, structured garbage out.

References

  • PM Skills Marketplace on GitHub
  • The Product Compass Newsletter
  • Claude Code Plugins Documentation
  • Teresa Torres — Continuous Discovery Habits
  • Marty Cagan — Silicon Valley Product Group

item.title: Stop Winging It: The 11-Step Workflow for Shipping Features with AI
item.content:encoded:
Stop Winging It: The 11-Step Workflow for Shipping Features with AI

You asked the AI to add a payment retry mechanism. It built a whole new payment service. You asked it to fix a date formatting bug. It refactored the entire date utility library. You asked for a simple API endpoint. It scaffolded a microservice with its own database.

Two minutes of AI generation. Two hours of cleanup.

If this sounds familiar, your AI coding assistant isn’t broken. Your process is missing.

The Real Bottleneck

Most developers interact with AI coding agents the same way: type a rough description of what they want, hit enter, and hope for the best. When the output misses the mark, they iterate with increasingly frustrated follow-up prompts. “No, not like that.” “I meant the other table.” “Why did you change that file?”

The pattern looks like conversation. It’s actually chaos.

The AI has no structured requirements. No acceptance criteria. No definition of what “done” means. No understanding of what it should not touch. You wouldn’t onboard a human developer this way. But somehow, when the developer is an AI, process goes out the window.

@123olp posted a thread on X (2026-03-03) that nails this problem and proposes a complete solution: a closed-loop workflow for quickly implementing requirements with AI. Not a vague “prompt engineering” guide. A real engineering process with gates, checkpoints, and rollback strategies.

Here’s the workflow, distilled.

The Two Roles

The workflow separates responsibilities cleanly:

You (requirement owner): Provide verifiable requirements. Make key decisions. Final approval on merge and release.

AI (executor): Produce Spec, task breakdown, implementation, self-testing, acceptance reports, and release materials.

The operating principle: one strong confirmation (Spec) + small-step commits + layered acceptance (P0/P1/P2) + always rollback-ready.

Step 0: Structured Requirement Input

This is where most people fail. They write “add retry logic for payments” and expect the AI to read their mind.

Instead, structure your input:

  • One-liner: “I want [X] so that [Y]”
  • Object/Scenario: Who uses it, where, what’s the main path
  • Constraints: Tech stack, APIs, things that must not change, permissions, launch window
  • Minimum Viable Acceptance (MVA): Checkboxes written as testable statements
    • “When [input], the system should [behavior]”
    • “When error/unauthorized/not-found, system should [fallback]”
    • “Must not affect [list 3-5 regression points]”
  • Out of scope: What you are explicitly not building

This takes five minutes. It saves hours.

Step 1: AI Produces the Spec

The AI generates a one-page executable Spec covering:

  1. Goals and non-goals
  2. User/caller behavior (main flow + key boundaries)
  3. Interface/data contracts (params, responses, error codes, idempotency, compatibility)
  4. Change scope (modules, files, blast radius)
  5. Risks and dependencies (external services, migrations, permissions, security, performance)
  6. Acceptance criteria (P0 must-haves + optional items)
  7. Rollback/degradation strategy (at minimum one actionable sentence)
  8. Deliverables checklist (code, tests, docs, PR description)

This is the only “strong confirmation point” in the entire workflow.

Step 2: You Confirm the Spec

Your response is binary:

  • “Spec OK, go.” or
  • “Spec needs changes: A, B, C”

Once you approve the Spec, you don’t need to confirm every step afterward. The AI executes autonomously unless it hits an exception gate (more on that below).

This single gate eliminates most rework. The AI and you are now aligned on what “done” means before a single line of code is written.

Step 3: AI Breaks Down Tasks

The AI produces 3-8 tasks, each with:

  • Task name (starts with a verb)
  • Inputs and dependencies
  • Files/modules to change
  • Deliverables (code, config, migrations, docs)
  • Self-test method (command, test case, expected result)
  • Definition of Done (lint, test, type-check requirements)
  • Rollback point (feature flag, config, compatibility path)

Step 4: Incremental Execution with Self-Checks

No “big bang” implementations. For each task, the AI outputs:

  1. What changed (summary + key diff explanation)
  2. Self-check results (lint, unit tests, logs, key test cases)
  3. Risk flags (newly discovered boundaries or dependencies)
  4. Next step

This rhythm catches problems early and produces reviewable evidence at every step.

Step 5: Exception Gates

During execution, the AI must stop and ask for your decision if any of these occur:

  • Requirement conflict or ambiguous interpretation
  • New dependency, permission, or data migration needed
  • External contract change (API, fields, behavior)
  • Significant risk escalation (security, performance, rollback infeasibility)
  • Scope creep beyond out-of-scope boundaries

After you decide, the AI updates only the affected Spec sections and continues.

These gates prevent the AI from silently going off-track. They’re the safety net that makes autonomous execution trustworthy.

Step 6: Layered Acceptance

The AI generates and executes a verification checklist with evidence.

P0 (always do):

  • Functional: main path + key boundaries (null, invalid, unauthorized, not-found)
  • Regression: the 3-5 points you specified in requirements
  • Automation: format/lint + critical unit tests or contract tests
  • Error handling: no swallowed errors, traceable logs, no sensitive data leaks
  • Compatibility: old behavior preserved (or explicit breaking change with migration guide)
  • Rollback: one-click revert available (flag, config, rollback steps)

P1 (recommended based on importance):

  • Performance: critical path latency, query count, large dataset behavior
  • Security: input validation, injection risks, permission boundary audit
  • Observability: metrics and instrumentation for production debugging

P2 (optional):

  • Extended test matrix (multi-platform, load testing, chaos)
  • Structural refactoring (only if obvious debt)

Step 7: Testing

Layered testing plan:

  • Local/unit: always
  • Integration: when interfaces, databases, or external dependencies are involved
  • Staging/canary: when real traffic or high-risk changes are involved

Step 8: Release with Guardrails

  • Feature branch development, never push directly to main
  • Small commits (1-2 per task)
  • PR includes: change summary, test evidence, risk points, monitoring notes, rollback steps, migration scripts if applicable
  • Merge only when CI green and P0 passes
  • Canary deployment for risky changes: flag on, small traffic, then full rollout

Step 9: Post-Launch Verification

After deployment, verify against the Spec’s acceptance criteria:

  • Smoke test critical paths
  • Monitor logs and metrics for anomalies (error rates, latency, key indicators)
  • If anomalies: execute rollback plan, document root cause

Step 10: Definition of Done

A requirement is “done” when all of these are true:

  • All P0 acceptance criteria pass
  • PR merged and deployed
  • Rollback plan verified (exercised or at minimum executable)
  • Post-launch verification complete
  • Records linked: PR, test report, acceptance report

Why This Works

The workflow succeeds because it solves the actual problem: not AI capability, but the absence of structure around AI execution.

One strong gate (Spec) aligns intent. Small steps catch drift. Exception gates prevent silent failures. Layered acceptance makes verification practical rather than exhaustive. Rollback readiness makes shipping safe.

The AI becomes a disciplined executor rather than a chaotic code generator. You stay in the driver’s seat without micromanaging every line.

Try It Tomorrow

Pick your next small feature. Write the structured requirement input. Let the AI produce the Spec. Confirm it. Watch the difference.

The workflow comes from @123olp’s thread on X (2026-03-03). It’s practical, battle-tested, and works with any AI coding assistant — Claude Code, Cursor, GPT, or whatever ships next.

Structure your input. Confirm once. Let it run.

Source: @123olp on X (2026-03-03)

item.title: Your AI Agent Skills Are Broken. Here's the Fix.
item.content:encoded:
Your AI Agent Skills Are Broken. Here’s the Fix.

You’ve been writing skills wrong.

Not a little wrong. Fundamentally wrong. Most developers approach AI agent skills like documentation for a junior developer — dump some context, add headers, throw in examples, hope it works. The result: bloated context windows, misfiring triggers, inconsistent output.

Minko Gechev’s skills-best-practices repo treats this problem with the rigor it deserves. The core insight is simple and sharp: skills aren’t documentation. They’re programs. The runtime is an LLM.

The anatomy of a skill

Every skill follows one structure:

No README. No changelog. No library code. If a file doesn’t serve the agent at runtime, it’s waste.

SKILL.md is the brain — high-level procedures and navigation. References hold API docs and cheatsheets. Scripts handle fragile, repetitive operations where variation is a bug. Assets store templates the agent copies. That’s the entire surface area.

Discovery is where skills die

The agent sees exactly two things before deciding to load your skill: name and description. That’s 1,024 characters to determine whether your skill exists or doesn’t.

Bad description: “React skills.”

Good description: “Creates and builds React components using Tailwind CSS. Use when the user wants to update component styles or UI logic. Don’t use it for Vue, Svelte, or vanilla CSS projects.”

Notice the negative triggers. You have to tell the agent when NOT to fire. Without them, your Angular migration skill will activate on React projects. Your Python testing skill will trigger on Go code.

The name field has strict rules too: 1-64 characters, lowercase letters, numbers, hyphens only, no consecutive hyphens, and it must match the parent directory name. angular-testing lives in angular-testing/SKILL.md. No exceptions.

Context window is memory. Manage it.

The guide calls it “Just-in-Time Loading.” Don’t pull references into context until the agent needs them. Keep SKILL.md lean: procedures and navigation only. Move schemas, API docs, and cheatsheets to references/. Move templates to assets/. Tell the agent explicitly when to read each file: “See references/auth-flow.md for specific error codes.”

Flat subdirectories only. One level deep. references/schema.md works. references/db/v1/schema.md doesn’t. This keeps the mental model simple for both you and the agent.

This is memory management for a runtime that charges per token.

Write for machines, not humans

Three rules that change how you author skill instructions:

Step-by-step numbering. Define workflows as strict chronological sequences. Map decision trees explicitly: “Step 2: If you need source maps run ng build --source-map. Otherwise, skip to Step 3.” No ambiguity. No room for creative interpretation.

Concrete templates over prose. Agents pattern-match well. A JSON template in assets/ beats paragraphs describing what the output should look like. Don’t describe — show.

Third-person imperative. “Extract the text…” not “You should extract…” or “I will extract…” This framing produces more consistent agent behavior.

Pick one term per concept and stick with it. Use domain-native vocabulary. In Angular, say “template” — not “html”, “markup”, or “view.” Consistency reduces the chance of the agent conflating similar concepts.

Offload fragile work to scripts

Don’t ask an LLM to write complex parsing logic from scratch every time a skill runs. Give it a tested Python or Bash script in the scripts/ directory. Design scripts as tiny CLIs.

The critical detail: make scripts return descriptive, human-readable error messages on stdout/stderr. When a script fails with “Error: Unable to resolve CommonJS dependency ‘lodash’ — try adding it to the Vite optimizeDeps.include array,” the agent can self-correct. When it fails with exit code 1 and no message, the agent is stuck.

Don’t create files that waste tokens

Skills are for agents, not humans. The guide is explicit about what NOT to create:

  • Documentation files: No README.md, CHANGELOG.md, or INSTALLATION_GUIDE.md
  • Redundant logic: If the agent already handles a task reliably, delete the instruction
  • Library code: Skills contain tiny, single-purpose scripts. Long-lived libraries belong elsewhere

Every file in a skill directory consumes context. Be ruthless about what earns its place.

Test skills with LLMs

This is where the guide separates itself from typical “best practices” lists.

Discovery validation. Paste your YAML frontmatter into a fresh LLM chat. Ask it to generate three prompts that should trigger the skill and three that shouldn’t. If the description can’t discriminate between them, rewrite it. Then go further — prompt agents with real assignments and inspect which skills they chose and why.

Logic validation. Feed the LLM your entire SKILL.md and directory tree. Have it simulate execution step-by-step against a concrete task. For each step, it writes out what it’s doing, which file it’s reading or running, and — critically — flags every point where your instructions force it to guess. These are “execution blockers”: ambiguities that will produce inconsistent results in production.

Adversarial testing. Switch roles. Make the LLM attack your skill as a QA tester. “What if the esbuild script fails on a CommonJS dependency? What if the user’s angular.json has custom Webpack builders that Vite doesn’t support? What assumptions am I making about the Node environment?” Don’t fix anything yet — just collect the questions.

Architecture refinement. After answering the adversarial questions, have the LLM restructure your skill. Push dense content out of SKILL.md into references/ and assets/. Add error handling sections. Enforce progressive disclosure so the context window stays lean.

The guide also points to SkillsBench — a benchmark for evaluating skills — as inspiration for building your own evals.

The endgame

Gechev’s repo includes a meta-skill: a skill that creates other skills. That’s the direction this is heading. Agents writing instructions for agents, validated by agents.

We’re not there yet. But treating skills as first-class software artifacts — with architecture, testing, and deployment discipline — is the difference between an agent that occasionally helps and one that reliably ships.

Quick reference

PrincipleRule
StructureSKILL.md + scripts/ + references/ + assets/
SKILL.md sizeUnder 500 lines
SubdirectoriesFlat, one level deep
DiscoveryTrigger-optimized description with negative triggers
LoadingJust-in-time, not upfront
InstructionsThird-person imperative, step-by-step
TemplatesConcrete examples over prose descriptions
ScriptsTiny CLIs with descriptive error messages
ValidationLLM-driven: discovery, logic, adversarial, refinement
WasteNo README, no changelog, no library code

References

  • mgechev/skills-best-practices — The source repo
  • Claude Agent Skills Best Practices — Official documentation
  • SkillsBench — Benchmark for evaluating agent skills

item.title: AI-Native R&D Team Blueprint: Stop Bolting AI Onto Old Processes
item.content:encoded:
AI-Native R&D Team Blueprint: Stop Bolting AI Onto Old Processes

You installed Copilot. You bought Claude Code subscriptions. Someone on the team uses ChatGPT to write unit tests. You tell yourself, “We’re already using AI.”

But nothing has changed. Same processes. Same meetings. Manual testing. Last-minute all-nighters before release. AI became a faster pen, but you’re still writing the same way.

This post breaks down a 15-slide blueprint document — the “AI-Native R&D Team Blueprint.” It answers one question: what’s the actual gap between “bolting an AI plugin onto old processes” and “rebuilding your entire R&D pipeline around AI capabilities”?

The Gap Between “Add a Tool” and “Change How You Work”

The blueprint opens with two columns. Left: “AI-Assisted.” Right: “AI-Native.”

In AI-Assisted mode, AI is a personal productivity tool. Humans write code, AI autocompletes. Existing processes stay the same. Quality depends on manual effort. Scale by hiring more people.

In AI-Native mode, AI is the team’s core production capacity. Humans become definers and verifiers — the entire middle layer of execution goes to AI. Processes are redesigned around what AI can do. Quality relies on automated testing. Scale by adding tokens (trade compute for headcount).

These aren’t different points on the same spectrum. They’re two entirely different production models.

Linear Division of Labor Is Collapsing

R&D teams used to have a clear chain: Product → Development → Testing. Everyone guarded their segment.

AI broke that chain. Pure execution roles — people who only write code, only build pages, only run manual tests — are being replaced at scale. Not tomorrow. Now.

Two paths remain for the people who stay:

Move up. Architecture, product direction. System-level understanding, tech decisions, designing collaboration strategies between humans and AI.

Move deep. Quality definition, test strategy. From manual executor to the person who defines the quality baseline.

No third path. The middle ground is vanishing.

Product Managers: Writing PRDs Isn’t Valuable Anymore

This might sting.

Drawing prototypes, writing PRDs, generating solution documents — those used to be the core output of a PM. AI leveled execution ability. A junior PM can now produce a PRD with AI that looks structurally identical to what a five-year veteran writes.

So what’s still valuable? Judgment.

Deciding what to build and what not to. Checking whether the underlying logic holds. Anticipating technical debt. Having the discipline to say “we’re not doing this.” AI can’t do any of that.

The blueprint puts it bluntly: the most important skill is no longer getting things done — it’s deciding what things should be done.

The new PM method is called “define downward, judge upward.” Deliverables are no longer feature lists. They’re layered product definitions — Role, Journey, Story — plus value judgment at each layer. The clearer the bottom-level definition, the more solid the ground AI works on.

Developers: You Can’t Just Be a Coder Anymore

The blueprint draws the developer’s new work cycle as a loop: define clearly → drive AI generation → verify accuracy → submit PR/MR → continuous Code Review → back to clear definition.

Notice: “writing code” is no longer the core action. Defining and verifying are.

Three evolution paths:

Architecture evolution. System-level design, tech decisions, AI collaboration strategies.

Product integration. Deep business understanding. Independent product definition. Driving AI from definition to end-to-end delivery.

Quality depth. Quality engineering, test strategy definition, maintaining the quality floor for AI-generated code.

The execution layer uses tokens as leverage. But to use leverage well, you need stronger architectural comprehension — the ability to judge whether AI-generated code meets standards and maintains backward compatibility.

QA’s Transformation: From Click-Testing to Defining Quality Baselines

The blueprint draws a radar chart comparing traditional QA versus AI-native QA across five dimensions: development background, manual testing experience, business comprehension, domain language depth, and quality definition ability.

AI writes code fast, but there’s a trap: it produces the illusion of “passing tests just to pass.” Surface-level green. Underneath, logic gaps.

QA can no longer be manual executors. QA must become the people who define quality baselines. Having a development background — understanding code and frameworks — becomes a hard requirement.

Not about testing more. About testing precisely.

CTO vs. Architect: Clear the Division
